affordances.name: Segment offers by value evidence
affordances.mechanism: When different customer segments genuinely value the same outcome differently, use differentiated offer shapes, terms, or service levels only if segmentation evidence, trust boundaries, and arbitrage risks are explicit.

use_when: Different segments genuinely value the same outcome differently.
Offer shape, terms, or service levels can be separated cleanly enough to avoid buyer confusion.
Segmentation is grounded in willingness to pay, urgency, risk, or use case rather than shallow demographic stereotypes.
do_not_use_when: The source evidence is only a generic buyer story or demographic label.
Customers can arbitrage between tiers or easily compare unequal treatment.
Short-term extraction would damage long-term trust, brand clarity, or willingness to buy.
case_evidence_needed: The distinct segments and the value driver for each.
Evidence of willingness to pay, urgency, risk, or use-case difference.
The tier boundary and any arbitrage path.
A trust or brand-risk check for perceived unfairness.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to validate segment-specific value before recommending differentiated pricing or offers, and to check whether the structure erodes trust.
evidence_required: The intended tier, term, or service-level difference.
A buyer confusion or arbitrage check.
A long-term trust or brand clarity check.
good_output_shape: States the tier or offer boundary.
Adds unfairness, arbitrage, and trust checks.

diagnostic_questions: What evidence shows these segments value the same outcome differently?
Can buyers compare or arbitrage across tiers in a way that makes the structure feel unfair?
Does the pricing logic capture value while preserving long-term trust?
misuse_guards: Do not infer willingness to pay from demographics alone.
Do not use differentiated pricing as short-term extraction without trust analysis.
Do not hide a generic pitch behind segmentation language.
Do not treat persona, archetype, or buyer-psychology language as proof of willingness to pay.

source_evidence.source_quote: different segments genuinely value the same outcome differently
segmentation is grounded in willingness to pay, urgency, risk, or use case
customers can arbitrage between tiers or compare treatment easily

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 16/PR88 outcome: thin_narrow_affordance_record. The source says the term is not explicitly defined, but supports a narrow differentiated-offer affordance with segment-evidence, arbitrage, and trust caveats.
dropped_material: Did not extract a complete economics doctrine of price discrimination.
Moved reference-alternative and lower-tier-limit material into a separate weak-support affordance because it has a distinct receiver transaction.
Did not treat buyer psychology as license for manipulation.
open_questions: A stronger economics source may be needed before this card should carry high-confidence pricing advice.

affordances.name: Justify offer differences against alternatives
affordances.mechanism: When a differentiated price, tier, or lower-cost offer is being justified, require the answer to compare it against a reference alternative, connect feature differences to concrete benefits or risk reduction, and state the limitations buyers must understand.

use_when: A higher-price, lower-price, or differentiated offer is being justified against an alternative.
The buyer needs to understand the benefit, risk reduction, or tradeoff behind the price or service-level difference.
A lower-tier or lower-cost option needs explicit limits so price comparison does not hide risk.
do_not_use_when: The justification relies on persona, archetype, or persuasion language without a reference alternative or benefit evidence.
A demand curve, price-response slope, or willingness-to-pay claim is inferred from story fit rather than observed response evidence.
The answer makes formal economics, legality, market-power, regulatory, or consumer-welfare claims beyond this weak source.
case_evidence_needed: The reference alternative or comparison offer.
The feature-to-benefit or monetary-value rationale for the difference.
The lower-tier limitation, risk tradeoff, or service-level boundary.
Evidence that the comparison preserves trust rather than manipulating perceived value.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to compare each differentiated offer against a reference alternative and make lower-tier limits or risk tradeoffs explicit, rather than relying on persona language alone.
evidence_required: The comparison alternative or reference offer.
The feature-to-benefit or value rationale behind the difference.
The monetary benefit, risk reduction, or urgency claim behind the price.
The limitations, risks, or service-level boundaries buyers need to understand.
good_output_shape: Answers compared to what before justifying the differentiated offer.
Connects tier differences to concrete benefits or risk reduction.
States lower-tier limitations plainly enough to reduce confusion and trust damage.
Keeps the card weak-support rather than treating comparison framing as economics doctrine.

diagnostic_questions: Compared to what alternative does the buyer judge this offer?
Which feature, benefit, monetary value, or risk reduction explains the difference?
What limitation or tradeoff must be explicit so the lower-price option does not become hidden risk?
misuse_guards: Do not use comparison framing as proof of willingness to pay.
Do not turn FUD or buyer psychology into pricing evidence without a concrete alternative.
Do not hide lower-tier limits behind value language.
Do not use this weak-support card for formal economics, legal, or regulatory price-discrimination claims.

source_evidence.source_quote: converts every product *feature* into a corresponding customer *benefit*
quantify the monetary benefits the product will deliver
challenge the assumption that a lower price can be achieved without incurring higher risk

review_notes.normalization_note: PR88 split out a weak-support offer-justification transaction from the original segment-separation card. The source still does not support full price-discrimination doctrine.
dropped_material: Did not promote comparison framing into proof of willingness to pay.
Did not extract formal first-, second-, or third-degree price discrimination claims.
open_questions: A stronger economics source may be needed before this card should carry high-confidence pricing advice.

absence_records.reason: The source requires willingness-to-pay, urgency, risk, or use-case grounding; it does not support differentiated pricing from segment labels alone.

source_evidence.source_quote: rather than in shallow demographic stereotypes

absence_records.reason: The source warns that short-term value extraction can damage trust and brand clarity; it does not support using willingness-to-pay evidence as unrestricted extraction license.

source_evidence.source_quote: damages long-term trust, brand clarity, or willingness to buy

absence_records.reason: The source treats personality frameworks and archetypes as useful guides, but the pricing affordance requires willingness-to-pay, urgency, risk, or use-case grounding; do not promote persona language or buyer psychology into pricing evidence by itself.

source_evidence.source_quote: personality frameworks (like the Big Five traits) are useful guides
segmentation is grounded in willingness to pay, urgency, risk, or use case

Do not use this weak-support record for formal economics, legality, market-power, monopoly pricing, consumer-welfare, first/second/third-degree price discrimination, or regulatory claims without a stronger source.

absence_records.reason: The price-discrimination source treats personas and buyer psychology as useful guides, but adjacent elasticity evidence warns that story-led demand assumptions can replace observed response curves. Do not infer demand curves, price-response slopes, or willingness-to-pay strength from persona language, archetype fit, or narrative segmentation without observed response evidence.

maximizing value extraction by precisely tailoring offers and pricing

dropped_material: No runtime, prompt, lane, packet, deterministic pressure, pricing policy, or user-facing change is implied.
